1:
πᾶσα ψυχὴ ἐξουσίαις ὑπερεχούσαις ὑποτασσέσθω — Pasa psykhē (every soul) is a Hebraism for every person. Exousiai hyperekhousin — governing authorities (exousia, G1849, granted authority/competency, not raw power; hyperekhousa, G5242, excelling/surpassing — those set above). Hypotassesthō (G5293, be subject to) is the military-administrative word for placing oneself in proper order under a command structure — not blind obedience but structural recognition of rank.

οὐ γάρ ἐστιν ἐξουσία εἰ μὴ ὑπὸ θεοῦ, αἱ δὲ οὖσαι ὑπὸ θεοῦ τεταγμέναι εἰσίν — The perfect passive tetagmenai eisin (have been arranged by God and remain so): the divine ordering is presented as a standing reality. The ground of the political submission is not the virtue of rulers but the divine ordering. The argument is structural, not evaluative.
2: ὁ ἀντιτασσόμενος τῇ ἐξουσίᾳ τῇ τοῦ θεοῦ διαταγῇ ἀνθέστηκεν — Antitassomenos (G498, to set oneself against) versus diatagē (G1296, ordinance/ordering). The one resisting the authority resists the divine ordering (anthestēken, perfect: has taken a stand against and remains in resistance). Anthistēmi (G436) used in perfect marks a stable condition of opposition. The consequence: heautois krima lēmpsontai — they will receive judgment upon themselves.
3: οἱ γὰρ ἄρχοντες οὐκ εἰσὶν φόβος τῷ ἀγαθῷ ἔργῳ ἀλλὰ τῷ κακῷ — The magistrate (arkhōn, G758, ruler, chief) is described by function: terror to evil, not to good. The idealized description (rulers commend good conduct, punish bad) is not a naïve claim that all rulers behave this way, but an account of the institutional role rulers are designed to fulfill — the norm against which aberrations are measured. Theleis de mē phobeisthai tēn exousian — do you wish not to fear? Then do good.
4: θεοῦ γὰρ διάκονός ἐστιν σοὶ εἰς τὸ ἀγαθόν — Diakonos (G1249, servant/minister): the ruler is described as God's servant (diakonos, not doulos — a minister of a function, not a personal slave). The same term appears for the Christian minister at 15:8. τὴν μάχαιραν φορεῖ — bearing the sword (makhaira, G3162, the short sword of execution and police power). The present tense phorei (G5409, habitually wear/bear) marks the sword as permanent equipment of the office. Ekdikos eis orgēn (G1558, avenger for wrath) — the civil authority legitimately executes the divine wrath deferred by private persons in 12:19.
5: ὑποτάσσεσθαι οὐ μόνον διὰ τὴν ὀργὴν ἀλλὰ καὶ διὰ τὴν συνείδησιν — Two grounds for submission: fear of punishment (dia tēn orgēn) and conscience (syneidēsis, G4893). Syneidēsis is the faculty of moral self-assessment, the reflexive awareness of one's own moral status. Paul elevates submission from mere self-interest (avoiding punishment) to moral obligation arising from conscience. The second ground implies the first is insufficient motivation by itself.
6: διὰ τοῦτο γὰρ καὶ φόρους τελεῖτε — Phorous telein (G5411, G5055, to pay tribute/taxes): tax payment is the concrete application of the submission principle. Leitourgoi gar theou eisin — for they are ministers of God (leitourgos, G3011, a word from public service and cultic ministry). The term elevates the civil magistrate to the status of public servant in the divine economy. Eis auto touto proskarterountes (persisting in this very thing): the participle suggests the continuous, full-time character of governing service.
7: ἀπόδοτε πᾶσιν τὰς ὀφειλάς — Apodote (G591, give back/render): the concept of debt-payment reappears from v.8. The fourfold list — phoros (tribute, direct tax on property), telos (G5056, indirect tax/toll on goods), phobos (G5401, fear/respect), timē (G5092, honor) — exhausts the categories of civic obligation. The last two are not financial but relational. The verse marks the outer limit of civic obligation before the greater debt is named in v.8.
8: μηδενὶ μηδὲν ὀφείλετε εἰ μὴ τὸ ἀλλήλους ἀγαπᾶν — The wordplay on opheilē (debt, G3782) from v.7: after paying all other debts, one debt remains permanently outstanding — mutual love. The infinitive agapan (to love) is the content of the single perpetual obligation. ὁ γὰρ ἀγαπῶν τὸν ἕτερον νόμον πεπλήρωκεν — the one loving the other has fulfilled the law. The perfect peplērōken (has fulfilled and the fulfillment stands) is strong: love is not partial-law but complete-law fulfillment.
9: τὸ γὰρ Οὐ μοιχεύσεις, Οὐ φονεύσεις, Οὐ κλέψεις, Οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις — Paul follows a non-Masoretic order (7th, 6th, 8th, 10th commandment) — the same sequence found in some LXX manuscripts, Luke 18:20, and James 2:11. The order reflects a textual tradition Paul knew. ἀνακεφαλαιοῦται ἐν τῷ λόγῳ τούτῳ — Anakephalaioo (G346, to sum up under one head) is a rhetorical term from the summary recapitulation at the end of an argument. The commandments are not repealed but gathered under a single head: love your neighbor as yourself (Lev 19:18).
10: ἡ ἀγάπη τῷ πλησίον κακὸν οὐκ ἐργάζεται — Plēsion (G4139, neighbor — the one near) receives the practical definition: love works no harm to the neighbor. The negative is absolute: ouk ergazetai (does not work/produce). πλήρωμα οὖν νόμου ἡ ἀγάπη — love is the fullness (plērōma) of law. The same word used at 11:12, 25 for the fullness of Gentiles: here love brings the law to its completion, not by abolishing it but by filling it out completely.
11: εἰδότες τὸν καιρόν, ὅτι ὥρα ἤδη ὑμᾶς ἐξ ὕπνου ἐγερθῆναι — Kairos (G2540, the appointed time, the decisive moment) — different from chronos (clock-time). Hōra (G5610, hour) narrows the urgency: the moment for waking from sleep has arrived. Nyn gar eggyteron hēmōn hē sōtēria ē hote episteu samen — our salvation is nearer now than when we believed. Engyteron (nearer, comparative) marks progressive proximity. Sōtēria (G4991, salvation) here has an eschatological referent: the final deliverance, not the initial reception of the gospel.
12: ἡ νὺξ προέκοψεν, ἡ δὲ ἡμέρα ἤγγικεν — Proekopsen (G4298, has advanced, is far gone — aorist): the night has progressed toward its end. Ēngiken (G1448, has drawn near — perfect): the day stands near and remains so. The metaphor coordinates with the sleep/waking of v.11. ἀποθώμεθα οὖν τὰ ἔργα τοῦ σκότους, ἐνδυσώμεθα δὲ τὰ ὅπλα τοῦ φωτός — The clothing metaphor: apothōmetha (throw off, strip off) the works of darkness; endysōmetha (put on, clothe ourselves with) the armor of light. The military hopla (G3696, weapons/arms) rather than mere clothing heightens the agonistic framing.
13: ὡς ἐν ἡμέρᾳ εὐσχημόνως περιπατήσωμεν — Euschēmonōs (G2156, becomingly, with good form): the word has social connotations — behavior that conforms to recognized standards of propriety. Daylight makes conduct visible and accountable. The three paired vices — kōmois kai methais (carousing and drunkenness), koitais kai aselgeiais (sexual immorality and licentiousness), eridi kai zēlō (strife and jealousy) — pair external excess with its social destructive counterpart.
14: ἐνδύσασθε τὸν κύριον Ἰησοῦν Χριστόν — The positive clothing command names the garment explicitly: the Lord Jesus Christ. Endysasthe (G1746, aorist middle: clothe yourselves) — the same verb as v.12's putting on the armor of light. Here the armor is identified with a person. The command picks up the baptismal imagery of Gal 3:27 ("all who were baptized into Christ have put on Christ"). καὶ τῆς σαρκὸς πρόνοιαν μὴ ποιεῖσθε εἰς ἐπιθυμίας — Sarx (G4561) here carries the Pauline ethical sense: the fleshly nature with its desires (epithymiai). Pronoia (G4307, forethought, provision): do not make provision for the flesh's desires — do not plan for or supply what the fleshly impulses demand.
